title: Pooling across Time and Space
concept: 3D pooling downsamples features across both spatial (height/width) and temporal (time) dimensions to reduce tensor size and computation.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the effect of increasing temporal stride?

options.text: It reduces the temporal resolution of the output
It increases the spatial resolution of the frames
It doubles the number of channels

exercises.explain: Yes! A larger temporal stride reduces the number of output steps along the time dimension.

cards.title: Shrinking the video cube
cards.body: Just like images, video tensors can quickly grow too large for a computer's memory. In 2D neural networks, we use pooling to shrink image dimensions. For video, we need to pool in 3D: shrinking height, width, and the stack of frames!

cards.title: 3D Pooling
cards.body: 3D Pooling (such as 3D Max Pooling) downsamples spatiotemporal feature maps. It extracts the maximum or average value from a small 3D window ($p_t imes p_h imes p_w$). This reduces size in both space and time.

cards.title: Summarizing a video clip
cards.body: Think of 3D pooling as summarizing a video. Spatial pooling is like turning a high-res page into a small thumbnail. Temporal pooling is like cutting a 10-second clip down to a 2-second highlight. 3D pooling does both at once!

cards.title: Max pooling 2x2x2
cards.body: A 2x2x2 Max Pooling layer takes a cube of 8 values (2 frames, 2 pixels high, 2 pixels wide) and outputs just the single largest value. This halves the height, width, and temporal depth of the tensor.

cards.title: Not always symmetric
cards.body: You might assume 3D pooling must always shrink time and space equally (like 2x2x2). But we often use 1x2x2 pooling! This shrinks spatial dimensions to extract local textures while keeping the full temporal resolution intact.

cards.title: Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Pooling
cards.body: Comparing pooling configurations in video networks:

left.text:
Shrinks time and space together
Reduces computations quickly
Can lose quick temporal details

right.text:
Shrinks spatial area only
Maintains full frame count
Preserves temporal resolution

exercises.prompt: What does a 3D pooling layer with size 2x2x2 do to a tensor's dimensions?

options.text: It halves the temporal length, height, and width
It halves the channels while leaving space unchanged
It reduces only the temporal length to zero

exercises.explain: Correct! A 2x2x2 pooling layer reduces the size of temporal, height, and width axes by a factor of 2.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An asymmetric 3D pooling layer of size 1x2x2 downsamples spatial dimensions but keeps the temporal size the same.
exercises.explain: True. A temporal pool size of 1 means no downsampling occurs along the frame axis.

exercises.sentence: If we want to keep all frame rate detail while shrinking image sizes, we should use a pooling kernel size of ___.
exercises.explain: A temporal kernel size of 1 keeps the time dimension unchanged, while 2x2 downsamples height and width.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a valid 3D pooling type commonly used to compress video representations?
exercises.explain: 3D Max Pooling is widely used to downsample spatiotemporal feature tensors.

exercises.prompt: Your video model has a bottleneck: it is too slow, but you cannot afford to lose any high-frequency motion details. Which pooling should you apply in the early layers?

options.text: 1x2x2 pooling, because it reduces spatial calculations while preserving all temporal frames.
4x4x4 pooling, to aggressively compress all dimensions.
2x1x1 pooling, to compress time and keep spatial resolution.
No pooling at all to maximize memory consumption.

exercises.explain: Exactly! 1x2x2 pooling shrinks the spatial dimensions (saving GPU memory) but doesn't downsample the frame axis, keeping all motion details safe. Next: how do we stack these layers into a full 3D CNN?
